title: Identifying How to End Relationships For each A and B pair, check the more effective response. • •

Interpersonal Effectiveness Handout 13a
Identifying How to End Relationships
For each A and B pair, check the more effective response.
•
•
•
•
•
•
•
•
•
•
•
•


 1A. If a relationship is threatening
your integrity or physical well-
being, it is probably your fault,
and you should see a therapist.

 1B. A relationship threatening your
integrity or physical well-being
is destructive, and you should
consider getting out of it.
•
•
•
•
•
•
•
•
•
•
•
•
•
•

In the middle of an argument, you are so mad at
the other person you don’t want to have anything to
do with this person any more.

 5A. You should end the relationship right then!
You may forget all about how enraging the
person is if you wait.

 5B. You should get out of emotion mind and into
Wise Mind, and evaluate whether to stay or
leave the relationship.
•
•
•
•
•
•
•
•
•
•


 2A. Relationships should be
easy. If it’s hard to have a
relationship with someone, it’s
probably not worth it, and you
should end it.

 2B. Most relationships need
problem solving to work.
•
•
•
•
•
•


 6A. If ending a destructive relationship will be
difficult, it’s most effective to stay together.

 6B. If ending a destructive relationship will be
difficult, it’s most effective to cope ahead of
time.
•
•
•
•
•
•
•
•
•
•


 3A. If you are in love with someone
who does not love you back,
practice DEAR MAN skills to
get the person to love you.

 3B. If you are in love with someone
who does not love you back,
practice opposite action to love.
•
•
•
•
•
•
•
•
•
•


 7A. In an abusive relationship, if the person
hits you, you should use your interpersonal
skills to tell the person you are leaving the
relationship.

 7B. In an abusive relationship, you should
seek professional assistance to leave the
relationship.
•
•
•
•
•
•


 4A. To decide whether to end a
relationship, do pros and
cons.

 4B. To decide whether to end a
relationship, use GIVE skills.
•
•
•
•


 8A. If you feel consistently invalidated in a
relationship, it is probably your fault.

 8B. If you are consistently invalidated, the
relationship is likely destructive.

ourspace_shared_life_filter.title: Identifying How to End Relationships For each A and B pair, check the more effective response. • •
ourspace_shared_life_filter.shared_facing_summary: Use this to protect both people: needs are real, boundaries are kind, and a pause is not abandonment when repair is named.
tiny_steps: Name the ask in one sentence.
Agree on a return time or next check-in.

flare_day_versions.minimum: One breath, one label, one safe message, or one checkbox.
flare_day_versions.medium: Two to five minutes with everything reachable from bed/chair.
flare_day_versions.full: Use the original worksheet/handout when both bodies and brains have capacity.

accessibility_modifications: Use text/chat if speech is hard.
Include caregiver capacity as part of the plan, not an afterthought.
relationship_and_caregiver_notes: William's needs and Jasper's capacity both count.
Ask before helping; ask before escalating; name the smallest next step.
Pause means pause, not abandonment. I am coming back. I care about us. I need a smaller next step.
What is needed, what capacity exists, what can be smaller, and what can wait?
identity_and_trauma_filter: Use William's masculine language and Jasper's they/them language.
Use consent-based, non-shaming, body-neutral language unless a person has explicitly chosen a term.
Do not turn symptoms, dysphoria, trauma responses, clutter, fatigue, or emotional intensity into moral failure.
avoid_or_adjust: Do not use skills to win, corner, threaten leaving, or make either person prove pain or disability.
